blocks.speaker: narrator
blocks.text: This is a story about a night you've already told. You'll tell it again here.

blocks.speaker: narrator
blocks.text: Partway through, you'll find out they've been telling a different version. This game is about the space between the two.

blocks.speaker: narrator
blocks.text: There is no verdict. You can stop whenever.

choices.label: Begin.
Begin (skip this next time).

blocks.speaker: narrator
blocks.text: A mutual friend mentioned it last week. Casually. The way people drop things they don't realize are grenades.

blocks.speaker: narrator
blocks.text: Your ex has been talking about that night. The last one. They have a version of it.

blocks.speaker: narrator
blocks.text: You don't know all of it yet. You know enough to know it doesn't match yours.

blocks.speaker: brain
blocks.text: Your first instinct was to correct the record. Your second instinct was to wonder if you could.

choices.label: What do you remember first?

blocks.speaker: narrator
blocks.text: Not the whole night. Just what surfaces first when someone says: that night.

choices.label: A fight. Something was said that couldn't be unsaid.
Quiet. The kind that means something has already been decided.

blocks.speaker: narrator
blocks.text: You've told this story before. Not to many people. Enough that it has a shape now.

blocks.speaker: narrator
blocks.text: You know which parts you lead with. Which parts you leave out.

blocks.speaker: brain
blocks.text: The leaving-out never felt like lying. It felt like editing for relevance.

choices.label: They escalated it. I was reacting.
We both said things.
They had already checked out. I was the last to know.
I think I already knew it was over. I just didn't say it first.

blocks.speaker: narrator
blocks.text: The kitchen. A specific quality of light — yellow, late. You both looked like you hadn't slept.

blocks.speaker: narrator
blocks.text: They said something that landed wrong. You said something back. Then it wasn't about the original thing anymore. It became about everything, the way those conversations do.

blocks.speaker: narrator
blocks.text: You remember the pitch of their voice near the end. The way they stopped looking at you and started looking past you.

blocks.speaker: body
blocks.text: You thought: they've rehearsed this.

blocks.speaker: brain
blocks.text: Whether that was true is a different question.

choices.label: That's the version you've been carrying.

blocks.speaker: narrator
blocks.text: You remember sitting across from them and feeling the absence before anything was said.

blocks.speaker: narrator
blocks.text: There was a meal, or the remnants of one. You were both pretending the conversation was normal. You were both bad at it.

blocks.speaker: narrator
blocks.text: At some point they said your name. Not to get your attention — just said it. Like they were testing whether it still meant something.

blocks.speaker: body
blocks.text: You answered. You don't remember what you said.

blocks.speaker: brain
blocks.text: That bothers you more than it should.

choices.label: That's the version you've been carrying.

addMemory.label: Quiet. Dinner. Your name, said like a test.

blocks.speaker: narrator
blocks.text: Here's what the mutual friend told you they said:

blocks.speaker: narrator
blocks.text: That you were already gone before the night started. That they were the one trying.

blocks.speaker: narrator
blocks.text: A sentence and a half. Secondhand. But enough to know that in their version, the roles are switched.

blocks.speaker: brain
blocks.text: You don't know if they're rewriting it or if they actually remember it that way. Those are very different problems.

choices.label: That's not what happened.
You sit with it before deciding what to do with it.
Something in it lands. You don't want it to.

scenes.title: What to do with a version you didn't ask for

blocks.speaker: narrator
blocks.text: Their version is out there. You can't un-know it.

blocks.speaker: narrator
blocks.text: The question is what you do with it.

choices.label: They're rewriting history. People do that when they need to.
Look for where both versions might be true at once.
Maybe yours is the rewrite.

blocks.speaker: narrator
blocks.text: The mutual friend told you one more thing. Specific enough that it stayed.

blocks.speaker: narrator
blocks.text: They said you went cold before they did. That by that night you were treating them like a problem to manage. That they felt like they were talking to someone who had already left the room.

blocks.speaker: narrator
blocks.text: You sit with this.

blocks.speaker: brain
blocks.text: The unsettling part isn't that it's wrong. It's that you can construct a version of the night where it's true.

choices.label: I wasn't cold. I was protecting myself.
Maybe. But that wasn't the whole picture.
You were. You know you were.

blocks.speaker: narrator
blocks.text: You go back into the night. Looking for something — evidence, maybe, or just the version that existed before theirs contaminated it.

blocks.speaker: brain
blocks.text: Memory doesn't work like that. But you try anyway.

choices.label: They came in ready to fight. I was the one trying to slow it down.
I don't know who started it. I know how I felt.
I was present. They were the one somewhere else.
I'm not sure anymore where I was.

blocks.speaker: narrator
blocks.text: You remember the moment it shifted. Before that it was an argument. After that it became something else.

blocks.speaker: narrator
blocks.text: They said: 'You never actually wanted this.' Not as a question.

blocks.speaker: narrator
blocks.text: You said something back. You don't remember the exact words — only the feeling behind them. A cold clarity. Like you'd stopped trying to reach them and started trying to win instead.

blocks.speaker: brain
blocks.text: That's the part you leave out when you tell it.

choices.label: You still leave it out.

addMemory.label: Cold clarity. You stopped trying to reach them.

choices.label: You sit with it. That part happened.

addMemory.label: You stopped trying to reach them.

blocks.speaker: narrator
blocks.text: You remember a specific silence. Not an absence of sound — you were both talking. But underneath, nothing.

blocks.speaker: narrator
blocks.text: At some point they reached for your hand across the table. You let them take it. You didn't pull away.

blocks.speaker: narrator
blocks.text: But you didn't hold on either.

blocks.speaker: brain
blocks.text: You've thought about that gesture. What it meant that you let them take your hand and felt nothing in particular.

choices.label: You were exhausted. That's different from gone.

addMemory.label: The hand. You let them. You didn't hold on.

choices.label: Maybe you were already gone.

blocks.speaker: narrator
blocks.text: You find out more. Not by asking — you wouldn't give them that. But it filters back.

blocks.speaker: narrator
blocks.text: They've told it to at least two people. In their version, they tried until the end. They say they asked you directly: do you still want this? They say you didn't answer.

blocks.speaker: narrator
blocks.text: You think: that never happened. Then: does my not remembering it mean it didn't, or just that I can't access it?

blocks.speaker: brain
blocks.text: There's a difference between a false memory and a lost one. You're not certain which this is.

choices.label: It didn't happen. You'd remember a question like that.
You genuinely don't know.
It sounds like something you would have done.

blocks.speaker: narrator
blocks.text: Two versions of the same night. Both told by someone who was there. Both internally consistent.

blocks.speaker: brain
blocks.text: The problem with memory isn't that it lies. It's that it's sincere.

choices.label: One of these is closer to the truth.
Both are partial. That doesn't make either of them wrong.
The actual night exists somewhere neither version can reach.

blocks.speaker: narrator
blocks.text: Set their version aside for a moment.

blocks.speaker: narrator
blocks.text: Just yours. What did you do that night? Not what happened to you. What did you do?

blocks.speaker: brain
blocks.text: This question has two forms: the one where you answer it honestly, and the one where you wait for someone to tell you it wasn't your fault.

choices.label: I responded. I didn't start it.
I did some things I'm not proud of.
I withdrew. That was a choice I made.

scenes.title: The moment both versions hinge on

blocks.speaker: narrator
blocks.text: Both versions return to the same moment. Each of you has named it as the turn.

blocks.speaker: narrator
blocks.text: In yours, it's the moment they made the choice. In theirs, it's the moment you did.

blocks.speaker: narrator
blocks.text: The moment: they asked if you were okay. Simple question. You said yes.

blocks.speaker: body
blocks.text: You were not okay.

blocks.speaker: brain
blocks.text: Neither version explains why you said yes.

choices.label: You said yes to protect them from something they couldn't have helped with anyway.

addMemory.label: You said yes. You were protecting them.

choices.label: You said yes because you'd already decided they weren't the right person to tell.

addMemory.label: You said yes. You'd already decided.

choices.label: You don't know why you said yes. You just did.

addMemory.label: You said yes. You don't know why.

blocks.speaker: narrator
blocks.text: The gap between your version and theirs is not going to close.

blocks.speaker: narrator
blocks.text: You've been looking for the explanation that makes one account true and the other a distortion.

blocks.speaker: narrator
blocks.text: Maybe that's not the right thing to look for.

choices.label: One of us is more right. I know which one.
Both versions are true for the person who lived them. That's the problem.
Maybe what actually happened belongs to no one.

blocks.speaker: narrator
blocks.text: Later you replay both versions. Parallel.

blocks.speaker: narrator
blocks.text: In yours: you were present. Trying. Hurt by something they were doing that they didn't know they were doing.

blocks.speaker: narrator
blocks.text: In theirs: you were already elsewhere. Decided. Leaving without saying so.

blocks.speaker: narrator
blocks.text: You can see both. That's the uncomfortable part.

blocks.speaker: brain
blocks.text: What you remember most honestly is this: you don't know what they were experiencing. You only ever had access to yourself.

choices.label: Continue.

blocks.speaker: narrator
blocks.text: You leave with something. You're not sure it's a conclusion.

choices.label: Continue.
Continue.
Continue.
Continue.
Continue.
Continue.

blocks.speaker: narrator
blocks.text: You stop trying to make one version win.

blocks.speaker: narrator
blocks.text: You hold both: the night you were trying and the night you'd already stopped. They overlap. They don't cancel each other out.

blocks.speaker: narrator
blocks.text: Their version of you is not you. But it's not nothing. It's what you looked like from where they were standing.

blocks.speaker: narrator
blocks.text: There's a kind of quiet in that. Not resolution — you won't call it resolution. The story just got bigger. Harder to carry, and somehow lighter.

blocks.speaker: brain
blocks.text: You don't have to agree with their version to let it matter.

choices.label: Start over with the other version.

blocks.speaker: narrator
blocks.text: Your version holds.

blocks.speaker: narrator
blocks.text: You were there. You know what happened. Their account is something they need — a story that makes the ending make sense for them. You understand that. You don't have to take it on.

blocks.speaker: narrator
blocks.text: The version you carry is coherent. It's been repeated enough that the shape feels permanent.

blocks.speaker: narrator
blocks.text: Somewhere, they're carrying theirs. You'll never compare notes.

blocks.speaker: brain
blocks.text: The wall keeps things out. It also keeps things in.

choices.label: Start over with the other version.

blocks.speaker: narrator
blocks.text: You find yourself adopting pieces of their version.

blocks.speaker: narrator
blocks.text: Not because you're certain they're right. Because doubt, held long enough, starts to fill in on the side of guilt.

blocks.speaker: narrator
blocks.text: You catch yourself telling the story differently now. Quieter about what they did. Less certain of your own account.

blocks.speaker: narrator
blocks.text: Maybe this is growth. Maybe it's a different kind of distortion — the self-punishing kind, which feels like honesty but isn't always.

blocks.speaker: brain
blocks.text: Accepting blame is not the same as telling the truth.

choices.label: Start over with the other version.

blocks.speaker: narrator
blocks.text: You stop.

blocks.speaker: narrator
blocks.text: Not because you've figured it out. Because you've run out of the kind of energy that trying requires.

blocks.speaker: narrator
blocks.text: The night sits there. Two versions, neither confirmed, both contaminated by the knowledge of the other.

blocks.speaker: narrator
blocks.text: At some point you stop calling it a question and start calling it a fact: something happened, you were there, and that's all you can say with any certainty.

blocks.speaker: brain
blocks.text: Not every story gets an ending. Some of them just stop.

choices.label: Start over with the other version.
